title: Learning to Take Up Space After Years of Shrinking
researcherTopic: C-PTSD, the four F types, emotional flashbacks
metaDescription: Explore why complex trauma teaches us to shrink and how to begin the journey of taking up space again. Learn to move from hypervigilance to embodied presence.
imageDescription: A single, delicate flower bud begins to unfurl in the warm morning light. The surrounding space is soft and out of focus, emphasizing the gentle, courageous act of opening. The scene evokes a sense of quiet expansion and the beauty of a slow, deliberate bloom.
finalH2: The Slow Thaw of Embodied Presence
htmlContent:
The air in the new apartment is thick with the ghost of paint and the low, constant hum of a refrigerator that feels both alien and eternal. It has been three weeks, and the boxes are mostly gone, but a certain kind of compression remains in the architecture of your own body, a holding pattern learned so long ago it feels like a part of your own skeleton. You stand in the middle of the living room, arms crossed not against a chill, but against the sheer, unnerving expanse of the space itself ~ a space you chose, a space you pay for, a space that still feels like it belongs to someone else, someone larger.

The Architecture of Shrinking

For those of us who have navigated the treacherous terrain of complex trauma, the act of making oneself small is not a conscious choice but a deeply ingrained survival strategy, a form of somatic architecture built brick by invisible brick in the earliest years of life. It is a response to an environment where our needs were too loud, our emotions too inconvenient, our very presence a disruption to a fragile and unpredictable system. This shrinking becomes a home, a familiar if cramped container that we carry with us long after the original threat has passed. In my years of working in this territory, I've seen how this internal blueprint dictates everything, from the way a person apologizes for their own existence to the way they hesitate before speaking in a meeting, a lifetime of yielding the floor even when it is rightfully theirs.

The Body as a Compressed File

Think of the body as a compressed file, a zip folder containing years of unprocessed experiences, unspoken words, and unfelt emotions, all condensed down to save psychic space. This compression is metabolically expensive, requiring a tremendous amount of life force to maintain. It emerges as chronic muscle tension in the jaw, the shoulders, the belly; it shows up as shallow breathing that never quite reaches the diaphragm; it becomes a pervasive sense of fatigue that no amount of sleep can seem to touch. This is the physical result of a life lived on high alert, a body braced for an impact that is always anticipated but never arrives. The work of healing, then, is not about adding more information or analysis, but about a careful and titrated process of decompression.

Permission is Not the Point

Many of us wait for an external signal, a clear and unambiguous permission slip from the universe granting us the right to take up space. We look for it in the approval of our partners, the praise of our bosses, the validation of our friends. We believe that if we can just be good enough, perfect enough, or compliant enough, we will finally earn the right to exist without apology. But this is a fundamental misunderstanding of how this internal architecture works. The habit of shrinking was created in the absence of permission, and it cannot be undone by seeking it externally. The search for permission is just another form of looking outside ourselves for a sense of safety that can only be cultivated from within.

A memoir that many C-PTSD survivors deeply connect with is What My Bones Know (paid link).

When you begin this process, you will inevitably encounter resistance, both internal and external. The internal resistance is the voice of the inner critic, the protector part that learned long ago that being small was being safe. The external resistance may come from those who were comfortable with your smaller version, those who benefited from your inability to set boundaries. Meeting this resistance not with force, but with curiosity, is the key. Each layer of fear, each wave of guilt, each impulse to retreat is simply data. It is the body’s grammar, speaking the language of its own history.

Bear with me on this one.

The Grammar of Expansion

If the body has a grammar of contraction, it also has a grammar of expansion, a language of opening that we can learn to read and eventually to speak. What we find here is not about puffing up your chest or adopting a power pose, though there can be value in exploring the interplay between posture and state. It is a much more subtle and internal process. It begins with the breath. For years, the breath has likely been shallow, a quiet, hidden thing. The first word in the grammar of expansion is the conscious, deliberate act of breathing into the belly, of allowing the diaphragm to descend and the ribs to flare, of taking in the air as if you have a right to it.

As we become more fluent in this grammar, we can begin to experiment with movement and voice. This might be as simple as stretching in the morning and noticing the full extension of your limbs, or allowing your voice to have a little more resonance when you speak, not forcing it, but simply allowing it to emanate from a place deeper in the body. It is about reclaiming the physical and energetic space that is rightfully yours, not by pushing others out, but by fully inhabiting your own center. It is a slow, incremental process of recalibrating a lifetime of contraction toward a new baseline of gentle, grounded expansion.

A resource specifically for abuse survivors that many find supportive is The Courage to Heal (paid link).

From Hypervigilance to Vigilance

One of the hallmarks of a life spent shrinking is a state of constant, low-grade hypervigilance... a nervous system perpetually scanning the horizon for threat. It turns out to be the legacy of an environment where danger was unpredictable. The senses become finely tuned to the external world, tracking every shift in tone, every subtle change in facial expression, every potential sign of disapproval or abandonment. This is an exhausting way to live, leaving little energy for creativity, connection, or rest. The path toward taking up space involves a crucial shift from this scattered, anxious hypervigilance to a focused, grounded, and internal vigilance.

This work from hypervigilance to vigilance is the process of becoming your own safe harbor. It is the realization that the ultimate source of security does not lie in controlling the external environment or pleasing others, but in your unwavering commitment to stay present with yourself, no matter what arises. It is a significant act of self-compassion and a fundamental prerequisite for any healthy relationship, including the one you have with yourself. It is the slow, steady work of building a regulated nervous system that can handle the complexities of life without defaulting to the old patterns of collapse and contraction.

The Slow Thaw of Embodied Presence

Learning to take up space after years of shrinking is not a linear process. It is a slow thaw, a gradual un-tensing of muscles that have been clenched for decades, an incremental expansion of a container that was built to be small. There will be moments of expansion followed by moments of contraction, days when you feel bold and sovereign followed by days when the old impulse to disappear feels overwhelming. The goal is not to eliminate the contraction, but to hold it with a larger and more compassionate awareness. It is to notice the impulse to shrink without being consumed by it, to feel the old fear without obeying it as an absolute command.

You already know what needs to happen next. You have known for a while. The knowing is not the problem. The doing is the problem. And that distinction is where your entire life lives right now.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link).

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I feel so guilty or scared when I try to take up more space?

It turns out to be a completely normal and expected part of the process. The guilt and fear are the echoes of the original conditioning that taught you that being small was necessary for survival, connection, or love. When you start to expand, you are going against a lifetime of programming, and the nervous system often interprets this change as a threat. The key is to meet this fear not as a stop sign, but as an indicator that you are moving into new territory, offering reassurance to the younger part of you that is afraid.

Think about that for a second.

How can I practice taking up space in a way that feels safe?

Start small and in low-stakes environments. The practice doesn't begin by confronting a hostile boss, but by simply noticing the space you occupy while sitting in a quiet room. Practice feeling your feet on the floor, or allowing your breath to fill your belly. You might experiment with holding a particular posture for 30 seconds longer than is comfortable, or allowing your voice to have a bit more resonance when you leave a voicemail for a friend. These small, incremental experiments build the capacity for larger acts of expansion over time.

Will people leave me if I start setting boundaries and taking up space?

What emerges is a common and valid fear. The honest answer is that some relationships, particularly those built on your habit of self-effacement, may be challenged by your growth. People who are used to you being accommodating may resist the change. However, healthy, secure relationships will not only withstand this shift but will celebrate it. As you begin to occupy your own space more fully, you will naturally attract and cultivate relationships that are based on mutual respect and sovereignty, rather than on enmeshment or control.

A guided journal specifically designed for trauma recovery is Trauma Recovery Journal (paid link).

The process of reclaiming your space is not about pushing others away, but about creating a strong enough center to truly meet them. It is the paradox of presence: the more fully you inhabit your own being, the more available you become for genuine connection. It is the quiet, steady practice of tending to your own garden, knowing that the right things will bloom in their own time.
